Read full article: Minor league ballplayer from Cocoa arrested in fatal hit-and-run crash in Manatee CountyA former Atlanta Braves minor league baseball player from Cocoa is accused of causing a fatal crash on I-75 in Manatee County and fleeing the area. Jonathan Matos Morales, 18, was arrested Tuesday.
Woman carrying infant fatally struck on SR-46 in Seminole County, FHP says
Read full article: Woman carrying infant fatally struck on SR-46 in Seminole County, FHP saysAccording to FHP, the woman was walking westbound in the westbound travel lane, carrying a car seat with an infant, when a pickup truck also traveling westbound struck her from behind.
9 students hurt in hit-and-run crash with Marion County school bus

Read full article: 3-year-old struck, killed by reversing pickup truck in Ocala driveway, deputies sayAccording to FHP, a pickup truck was backing out of the driveway when it struck the child, who was standing behind the vehicle.

What else the bill mandatesFlorida lawmakers moved ahead Thursday with a proposal to get cyclists on e-bikes to slow down around pedestrians and make other safety improvements, although many suggested the effort should cover people using electric scooters as well.

article.title: 3 Motorcycle Riders Killed in I-595 Crash Involving Broward Sheriff's Deputy
article.text:
Authorities are investigating a crash on Interstate 595 in Broward County that left three motorcycle riders dead and involved a sheriff's office deputy.
The five-vehicle crash happened around 10 p.m. Wednesday in the westbound lanes of I-595 just east of Interstate 95.
Broward Sheriff's Office officials said a Yamaha motorcycle and Suzuki motorcycle collided with each other on the highway, knocking both bikes as well as the Suzuki rider and the Yamaha rider and his passenger to the ground.
After the initial collision, a Lexus car hit the Suzuki motorcycle, officials said.
Detectives believe the Suzuki rider was checking on the two people from the Yahama when he was struck by an unmarked Broward Sheriff's Office vehicle being driven by a deputy who was heading home after working a shift at the airport.
The three people who were on the motorcycles were pronounced dead at the scene. They were identified as Steven William Shearer, 25, of Fort Lauderdale; Cortney Taylor Walters, 28, also of Fort Lauderdale; and Edward Charles Benway III, 30, of Pompano Beach.
The deputy was taken to Broward Health Medical Center with non-life threatening injuries.
Local
Traffic homicide investigators said that there was limited visibility at night and a lack of time or distance produced because all vehicles were traveling at or about 65 mph.
The crash is being investigated and under BSO policy an internal affairs investigation will also take place, officials said.
article.author: NBC 6

article.title: Man describes traumatic scene of deadly pedestrian crash on Blanding Boulevard
article.text:
DUVAL COUNTY, Fla. – A 39-year-old pedestrian is dead after a crash on State Road 21, according to the Florida Highway Patrol.
Around 10:05 p.m. Friday, a 27-year-old woman driving an SUV was headed south on Blanding Boulevard when a man attempted to cross the street, north of Hicks Road.
The man walked into the path of the SUV and was struck, troopers said. He died at HCA Florida Orange Park Hospital.
A witness told News4JAX that the pedestrian was not using a crosswalk at the intersection of Hicks and Blanding but crossed at a median.
“He went from the median and he had to cross the turning lane, which had no vehicles in it, and from there when he stepped into the first travel lane, it was instant,” Patrick Walker said.
Walker told News4JAX the SUV driver was going the speed limit and would not have had time to stop.
A man who works at a smoke shop down the road also heard the impact.
“I heard an impact like it was a car hitting a car and then I looked over there -- you can see the reflection of the street, especially at night, and all I saw was the firetruck and the cops,” said Kyle Macnabb.
The driver, who was wearing a seat belt, was not injured, troopers said.
Jacksonville is sixth in the nation for pedestrian deaths, according to a report from Smart Growth America.
This isn’t the only fatal pedestrian crash that has happened recently on Blanding Boulevard.
According to FHP, in August, a pedestrian was fatally struck by a car off Blanding Boulevard at Parkwood Drive.
Troopers said the car had a green traffic light when the pedestrian stepped into its path and was struck by the sedan.
According to the Florida Department of Highway Safety and Motor vehicles Crash Dashboard, there have been 335 pedestrian crashes so far this year in Duval County and 32 deaths from those crashes.
article.author: Brie Isom; Bella Casapao

article.title: Pedestrian dies in Lauderdale Lakes crash
article.text:
A fatal pedestrian crash shut down northbound and southbound lanes of North State Road 7 in Lauderdale Lakes early Saturday morning.
A little after 4 a.m., a vehicle struck and killed a pedestrian near the intersection of Northwest 19th Street and North State Road 7, according to the Broward Sheriff’s Office. Deputies and Broward Fire Rescue responded, and the victim was pronounced deceased on scene.
The Sheriff’s Office announced that the roadway had reopened around 9:45 a.m.
Traffic homicide detectives investigated the crash, which was not a hit and run, according to Gerdy St. Louis, a spokesperson for the Broward Sheriff’s Office.
“All parties involved in the crash remained on scene,” St. Louis said.
No identities have been released.
article.author: Shira Moolten

article.title: Five Injured Following Multi-Car Crash in Broward County, officials say.
article.text:
Several people were trapped in their cars over the weekend in Broward County with at least five injuries, according to reporting from The Miami Herald.
The crash happened just after 2:30 p.m. Saturday, April 11, near the intersection of State Road 7 and Interstate 595. At least four cars were involved and trapped within their vehicles.
At least two people with serious injuries could not be identified and were taken to the hospital. One person went to Broward Health Medical Center and the other to Memorial Regional Hospital’s Ryder Trauma Center, the newspaper reported.
Additional information, including the conditions of the three remaining people involved in the crash and what caused the collision, were not immediately available Tuesday, April 14.
The investigation is ongoing.
Leesfield & Partners
Leesfield & Partners has been representing injured clients and their grieving families for 50 years throughout Florida. From devastating fatal crashes to horrific pedestrian cases, our attorneys work tirelessly to ensure the best possible outcome for every client. In decades of personal injury law, our attorneys have secured numerous verdicts and settlements on behalf of clients throughout some of the most complex cases in the state, earning the firm statewide and national recognition.
One such case involved a horrific head-on crash that forever shattered the lives of one family. The incident occurred while the family was on their way to drop off their daughter at the airport so that she could her medical school’s orientation. Due to the negligence of a drunk driver who was driving down the wrong side of I-95 and the bar that overserved him, this family suffered the tremendous and irreversible loss of their daughter. She was 23.
Also in the car was the family’s 21-year-old son. As a result of his injuries, the young man was left unable to speak, needing round-the-clock care and a wheelchair. The irreparable damage caused by the selfish actions of this individual forever changed this family’s life.
At trial, a jury awarded over $95 million to the family with $57 million going to the son and $38 million to the parents. This case represented the largest motor vehicle accident verdict in Miami-Dade County and was the fourth-largest accident-related verdict in state history.
“The result in this case shows what hard work and creative lawyering can do for clients with devastating injuries,” said Founder and Managing Partner Ira Leesfield. “Along with co-counsel Aaron Davis, we are happy to settle with two defendants and to obtain this record verdict for them.”
What to Do if You’re Injured in a Crash
- On the day of the accident, plaintiffs should ensure that they document as much as they can. This includes photographing the cars involved in the incident and photographing injuries.
- Crash victims should also write down exactly what happened and have everyone who was in the vehicle with them do the same.
- Be advised that your safety is paramount when collecting this evidence.
- Plaintiffs should seek medical attention straight away after being involved in a motor vehicle accident and collect copies of any medical records and doctor’s notes from these visits.
- Finding a personal injury lawyer who will take on your case with the necessary diligence, tenacity, and understanding of the law should not be a hasty process.
- Get recommendations from friends and family and do your research.
- Plaintiffs must notify both their own insurer and the at-fault party’s insurer. Their attorney will then send a demand letter outlining the defendant’s liability and the plaintiff’s injuries. The plaintiff’s attorney will engage in negotiations with the insurer or defense counsel. These discussions may lead to a settlement, allowing the plaintiff to resolve the claim without filing a formal lawsuit. If not, however, a lawsuit will be filed.
- If a resolution cannot be reached through settlement, the plaintiff’s attorney will file a complaint in civil court.
- Discovery, depositions and pre-trial motions will follow as the lawsuit continues. Depending on the specifics of the case, it may be resolved at trial or through a later settlement.
Previous Leesfield & Partners Cases
Partner Justin B. Shapiro previously represented a family in a wrongful death motor vehicle case involving a pedestrian. A verdict of over $36 million was obtained for the family in that case.
Mr. Leesfield was honored with the Trial Lawyers for Public Justice’s Lawyer of the Year award, following a $19.8 million verdict against Honda Corp, and was chosen as one of America’s Top Ten Trial Lawyers.
The $19.8 million verdict involved a defect with a Honda motorcycle’s kickstand, which deployed while our 27-year-old client was in the middle of a ride. As a result of the kickstand deploying, his motorcycle spun out of control and caused a terrible crash that left him paralyzed. This verdict was one of several involving several companies with kickstand defects that injured motorcyclists all over the country.
A $15.4 million recovery was obtained for a Fort Lauderdale handyman who was injured as a backseat passenger in a high-speed motor vehicle accident. The man in that case was in a car that was stopped at a drawbridge when another car hit it from behind, causing skull fractures, frontal lobe brain damage and facial fractures.
As a result of his injuries, our client developed a seizure disorder that could not be controlled by medication. This ended the then-22-year-old’s career as a handyman in the area.
Leesfield & Partners previously represented the families of three teenage girls after they were struck by a commercial truck driver making an illegal U-turn on the turnpike. The girls in that case were on their way to the library when the driver turned directly into their path. Tragically, one girl was killed and the other two sustained serious injuries.
Our attorneys worked tirelessly to settle the case for over $8.6 million for the families. The firm also obtained an over $7.6 million arbitration award for the family who lost their daughter.
Mr. Shapiro settled a pedestrian case for $6 million, the maximum recoverable amount. The crash happened in October 2019 when our client was walking back to her office after a midday break. While she was in the marked crosswalk in broad daylight, a pickup truck driver suddenly made an illegal left turn.
As a result, she suffered horrific skull fractures, brain hemorrhages and significant injuries to her left leg. The driver claimed not to have seen our client despite clear visibility, the marked crosswalk and road signage.
In another pedestrian-involved case, Leesfield & Partners obtained a $2.9 million recovery our client who was injured due to a driver’s negligence.
The firm previously settled a pedestrian case for $1.2 million. The pedestrian in this case was a child who was hit while attempting to cross a street in Fort Lauderdale. As a result of the crash, our minor client hit his head on the pavement and suffered a traumatic brain injury.
Previously, Mr. Shapiro represented a minor who was told to cross the street by a careless school bus driver just before he was struck in the road by a speeding driver. The boy suffered a traumatic brain injury as a result of the crash and had to be airlifted to the hospital. Through an investigation and identifying what could have been an overlooked defendant, Mr. Shapiro discovered that the bus driver violated state law and endangered the students she was tasked with protecting. By not stopping as far right as possible and by not ensuring 200 feet of visibility in either direction from her bus, this driver risked the lives of several students and our client.
A confidential settlement was reached in that case.
Trial Attorney Bernardo Pimentel II settled a $1 million fatal car accident case on behalf of a family who tragically lost their 8-year-old daughter due to the negligence of a pharmaceutical delivery company’s employee. In that case, the company attempted to argue that this employee was an independent contractor, however, Mr. Pimentel argue that because this company exerted a certain level of control over this driver, – wearing a uniform and issuing a schedule he was obligated to follow – their claim was invalid. This level of control categorized him as an employee making the company liable for his reckless driving.
Trial Lawyer Eric Shane recently obtained a confidential amount for a young passenger who was injured in a severe Florida crash.
Mr. Pimentel also settled a confidential case on behalf of a family whose 5-year-old son suffered serious injuries when he was hit by a car that crashed through the front of a Florida discount store.
Evan Robinson, a Leesfield & Partners Trial Attorney, previously secured a six-figure settlement on behalf of a client was injured in a Monroe County car accident.
If you or a loved one was injured in a motor vehicle accident in Florida, don’t wait. Call a Leesfield & Partners attorney today for a free consultation at 800-836-6400.

article.title: Multiple people killed in overnight crash on I-95 in Indian River County
article.text:
Multiple people killed in overnight crash on I-95 in Indian River County
Multiple people were killed in a crash on I-95 in Indian River County Monday morning.
The Indian River County Sheriff's Office did not confirm the number of victims but said the crash involved three vehicles.
The incident happened just south of County Road 512 around 2:22 a.m. Officials responded around 2:37 a.m.
The crash shut down northbound I-95 in Vero Beach for more than six hours.
The southbound lanes in the area were closed as well around 4:50 a.m. but were reopened shortly after.
As of 9:12 a.m., the northbound lanes remain closed between 20th Street and Fellsmere Road.
This is a developing story and will be updated as information becomes available.

article.title: Pasco County Man Dies After Being Hit by Car on US 19 in Hudson
article.text:
PASCO COUNTY, FLa- A 28-year-old Holiday man was killed Friday night after he walked into the path of a vehicle on US-19 in Hudson.
According to the Florida Highway Patrol, a Ford Focus driven by a 29-year-old man from Spring Hill was traveling southbound around 9:30 p.m. on US-19, north of Emerald Boulevard, when the 28-year-old Holiday man crossed the highway into the path of the vehicle.
The pedestrian suffered fatal injuries at the scene of the crash. The driver of the Ford Focus was not injured.
The name of the pedestrian has not been released. The cause of the crash is still under investigation.

article.title: Motorcyclist Dies in Crash on I-75 in Broward
article.text:
A motorcyclist is dead after crashing on I-75 in Pembroke Pines.
According to the Florida Highway Patrol, the single-vehicle crash involved a 41-year-old Hispanic man from Miami who was confirmed dead at the scene by Pembroke Pines Fire Rescue personnel. Officials did not release his name
Investigators said the man was driving north on Interstate 75 in the exit ramp only lane to westbound Pembroke Pines Boulevard when he was launched off the motorcycle.
FHP continues to investigate the cause of the crash.
Three right lanes and the Pines Boulevard off-ramp were shut down while authorities investigated.

article.title: FHP: 70-year-old bicyclist killed in hit-and-run crash on State Road 100
article.text:
BRADFORD COUNTY, Fla. — A man is dead after he was struck by a car that fled the scene on State Road 100 in Bradford County Monday afternoon, according to the Florida Highway Patrol.
FHP said around 2 p.m., the victim, a 70-year-old man from Crestview, was riding a bicycle heading south on SR-100.
A sedan entered the right shoulder of the roadway and crashed into the back of the bicycle.
FHP said the bicyclist was thrown onto the road, causing fatal injuries.
Troopers say the sedan involved in the crash left the scene traveling southbound on SR-100.
The suspect vehicle was later located, and charges are pending.

article.title: At least 1 person killed in Pasco County crash; eastbound SR-54 reopens
article.text:
PASCO COUNTY, Fla — A deadly crash shut down part of a busy road Monday morning in Pasco County, causing delays for commuters.
According to the Florida Department of Transportation, the crash happened around 7:20 a.m. on State Road 54 just before Gunn Highway. It appeared all eastbound lanes were closed at the crash site when Sky 10 flew over the area.
The lanes have since reopened to traffic.
Drivers were being detoured via a U-turn onto westbound SR-54. As a result of the crash, eastbound SR-54 was backed up for several miles.
Authorities have said the crash killed at least one person; however, it is unclear how many cars were involved and if there were additional injuries.
This is a developing story. Check back for updates.
article.author: Claire Farrow

article.title: Pickup truck driver dies in Baker County crash on SR 121
article.text:
Pickup truck driver dies in Baker County crash on SR 121
Florida Highway Patrol (WJXT, Copyright 2025 News4JAX - All Rights Reserved)
BAKER COUNTY, Fla. – A 61-year-old man died Sunday morning in Baker County after he veered off the road and overturned on State Road 121.
The accident report said the man was driving a pickup truck south on State Road 121 near Jerry Johns Road as he drove around a curve around 11 a.m.
For some reason, the report said, the man veered off the roadway and drove into a ditch, where he hit a concrete culvert, causing the vehicle to go airborne.
The pickup traveled into the woods and flipped over.
The driver, who the report said was wearing a seatbelt, died at the scene.

article.title: Crash claims two lives at dangerous US 19 intersection in Pasco County
article.text:
HUDSON, Fla. - Troopers with Florida Highway Patrol are investigating a crash that killed two people on US 19 in Pasco County.
It happened Wednesday evening near Beach Boulevard. Troopers say an SUV and a sedan were heading south on US 19 when the driver of the SUV lost control and crashed into a construction barrel, which then hit the sedan.
The driver of the sedan lost control and veered into oncoming traffic. When an oncoming car swerved to avoid hitting the sedan, it hit the SUV instead.
The SUV rolled over and came to a stop on the roadway.
The driver of the SUV, an 81-year-old woman from Virginia, suffered serious injures and was taken to the hospital. Her passenger, a 52-year-old Hudson man, died at the scene of the crash.
The driver of the car that crashed into the SUV, a 54-year-old Hudson man, died after being taken to the hospital. The driver of the sedan was not injured.
Meanwhile, construction barrels can be seen all around the intersection, where nearby drivers say a much-needed red light is being installed.
People in the area have counted 10 deadly accidents recently.
Law enforcement has long warned of the danger on US 19 and the Florida Department of Transportation said it is doing things to make the road safer, like installing pedestrian crossing areas and red lights.
Troopers haven’t released the names of the people involved in the accident.
article.author: Dan Matics

article.title: JSO: 1 dead, 1 injured after car crashes into tree in San Jose area
article.text:
JACKSONVILLE, Fla. — One person is dead and another is injured after a crash Tuesday night in the San Jose area.
According to the Jacksonville Sheriff's Office, a stolen Toyota Camry driven by a man in his twenties with two 16-year-old passengers was traveling north on Powers Avenue. Police say the man was driving recklessly at a high rate of speed.
At about 10:15 p.m., the driver lost control of the car in the 7200 block of Powers Avenue and hit a tree on the east side of the road, JSO said.
The driver was pronounced dead at the scene. He is the ninth person to die this year in a traffic-related incident in Duval County.
First responders took one of the passengers to the hospital with life-threatening injuries.
The second passenger was not injured and was detained by JSO.
According to JSO, the car was stolen on Jan. 16 from the Art Museum area.
Investigators say there is evidence that the driver was impaired at the time of the crash.

article.title: Man killed in motorcycle crash off I-75 in Riverview
article.text:
RIVERVIEW, Fla. - A man is dead after a motorcycle crash at a busy Interstate 75 interchange in Hillsborough County.
According to the Florida Highway Patrol, the crash happened around noon Sunday on Gibsonton Drive in Riverview.
FHP says the motorcycle rider, a 29-year-old man, was driving eastbound on Gibsonton Drive when the driver of a pickup truck exiting I-75 turned into his path.
The man died at the scene. His name has not been released.
The driver of the pickup, a 59-year-old woman from Gibsonton, suffered minor injuries.

When questioned, the girls said the Tesla was driving itself in autopilot mode when it backed into the patrol car. The school bus driver was not able to determine the race or gender of the driver nor the make and model of the car. An 18-year-old and 12-year-old were on the bus at the time of the crash Monday afternoon. Anyone who may have witnessed the crash that can provide additional information is asked to contact the Florida Highway Patrol Station at 904-417-4272.

Troopers said the 52-year-old man was behind the wheel of a sedan heading east on SR 100 when it crossed over the center line into the westbound lane and struck a box truck. Authorities are investigating why one vehicle, which they believe was a pickup truck traveling at a high rate of speed, crossed the center line about 9 p.m. and struck a small passenger car head-on. Deputies said a full-size pickup truck going the wrong way on State Road 100 at Southeast 109th Street just before dawn ran into a small car. Driver Laura Wilbert, 35, and her son, Christian Okrasinski, 16, both of Starke, were also killed, troopers said. The truck went airborne, flipped over and landed right where you're at right now," said a witness who didn't want his name used. All that's left behind is a bent street sign marking the intersection and deep tire tracks in the grass.

Both directions of SR 100 were closed at 8:36 a.m. after Andrew Garnett, of Keystone Heights, flipped his Ford Ranger, troopers said. It’s unknown why Garnett drove off the road. article.title: News: Woman facing DUI charge following fatal wreck on SR 35 in Marion County
article.text:
MARION COUNTY, FL – A car accident involving an alleged drunk in Marion County killed a passenger in the vehicle, reports WKMG.
The Florida Highway Patrol was dispatched to the fatal wreck early Saturday morning along State Road 35 near SE 102 Place Road in Belleview. The FHP’s preliminary investigation indicated that the driver of a sedan failed to negotiate a curve in the roadway while heading south on S.R. 35 at about 2:30 a.m.
The car went off the road and onto the grass shoulder before crashing into a wooden fence and a large tree.
A passenger, a 27-year-old Ocala man, was ejected from the vehicle and died after being taken to an area hospital. The driver, a 28-year-old woman from Belleview, was arrested to face a charge of DUI manslaughter. She sustained minor injuries in the crash.
It was believed that the woman was wearing a seat belt but the man was not, state troopers said.

article.title: One Dead and One Critical After Multi Vehicle Chain Reaction Crash on I 95 in Brevard County - The Space Coast Rocket
article.text:
A late night chain reaction crash involving five vehicles and a pedestrian left one man dead and another critically injured early Friday morning on Interstate 95 in Brevard County. The Florida Highway Patrol responded to the scene just after midnight at mile marker 223, south of State Road 46.
According to FHP, the crash unfolded in three separate impacts, beginning with a lane change gone wrong that set off a series of collisions across all three northbound lanes.
First Crash
Troopers say a 33 year old man from Port St. Lucie was driving a 2019 Chevy Impala (Vehicle 1) in the center lane when he attempted to move into the outside lane. The maneuver put him directly in the path of a 2024 Volkswagen ID.4 (Vehicle 2) driven by a 45 year old man from Weston.
The front right of the Impala struck the rear left of the Volkswagen. The impact caused the Volkswagen to veer off the roadway into a ditch. The Impala swerved across the inside shoulder and hit the concrete median before coming to a stop in the inside northbound lane.
The driver of the Impala, listed as Vehicle 1, was pronounced deceased on scene. His next of kin has been notified.
Second Crash: Good Samaritan Hit While Trying to Help
Following the first impact, a 51 year old driver from Ponte Vedra (Vehicle 3, a 2025 Hyundai Elantra) and a 23 year old woman from Cocoa (Vehicle 4, a 2019 Audi A4) were traveling northbound and approached the disabled Impala in the roadway.
FHP says the Ponte Vedra driver stopped on the inside lane and got out to help. He walked toward the disabled Impala. At the same time, the Audi swerved left and sideswiped the Elantra.
The Elantra was then pushed into the pedestrian, striking him and throwing him into the inside lane ahead of his own vehicle. The Audi also struck the pedestrian after swerving.
The pedestrian, listed as Pedestrian 1 in the report, was critically injured. He was airlifted to HCA Lake Monroe Hospital and remains in critical but stable condition.
Both drivers in this crash were wearing seatbelts and were not injured.
Third Crash
A fifth vehicle, a 2010 Nissan Murano driven by a 30 year old man from Coronado, California, then approached the scene. The Murano was traveling in the center lane when the pedestrian attempted to move out of the roadway.
The Murano driver swerved left but still struck the pedestrian a second time. The driver, passenger, and vehicle occupants were not injured and remained at the scene.
Casualties and Injuries
Deceased: 33 year old male, Port St. Lucie, driver of Vehicle 1 Critical: 27 year old pedestrian from Dallas, North Carolina Minor injuries: Driver of Vehicle 2 transported to Parrish Medical Center No injuries: Drivers of Vehicles 3, 4, and 5 and passenger in Vehicle 5
All individuals reported to be wearing seatbelts were properly restrained.
Investigation Ongoing
Troopers have not released the names of the individuals involved. The Florida Highway Patrol states that the investigation remains active.
Traffic on I 95 northbound was heavily impacted during the overnight hours as emergency crews responded, investigated, and cleared the scene.
More information will be released as the investigation continues.
article.author: The Space Coast Rocket

MARION COUNTY, Fla. – An Ocala man was killed and three others, including a 7-year-old girl were injured, in a head-on crash Sunday night in Marion County, the Florida Highway Patrol said.
The fatal wreck happened around 8:30 p.m. on State Road 40, east of County Road 314.
Recommended Videos
The FHP said the 52-year-old man was driving a pickup truck west on S.R. 40 and crossed over the center line and struck a car traveling east.
The man, who was not wearing a seat belt, was pronounced dead at the scene, according to troopers. His passenger, a 48-year-old Ocala woman, suffered critical injuries, an FHP crash report stated.
The driver of the other vehicle, a Deltona woman, and her passenger, a 7-year-old Deltona girl, were seriously injured, the FHP said. They were both wearing seat belts, the FHP report stated.
The women were taken to a hospital and the girl was transported to Shands UF for treatment, according to troopers.

article.title: Florida Personal Injury News | Dannheisser Injury Law
article.text:
A fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident happened Tuesday evening, February 10, 2026, near Venice at the intersection of State Road 681 and En Theos Lane. According to reporting citing the Florida Highway Patrol, a van entered the intersection as a motorcycle approached a yellow flashing signal, and the vehicles collided. The motorcyclist, described as a 25-year-old man, was pronounced dead at the scene, and the crash remains under investigation. In tragedies like this fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident, families are often left with grief, unanswered questions, and urgent decisions that can affect their rights.
If you are reading this because your family is trying to make sense of a similar fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident, the sections below walk through what has been reported, what investigators typically look for, and what options families often consider after a sudden loss. If you would rather talk with someone now, you can also contact Dannheisser Injury Law directly.
What happened in this fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident
Local reporting states the crash occurred around 6:33 p.m. on February 10, 2026, at the intersection of SR 681 and En Theos Lane in Sarasota County. The van was driven by a 32-year-old man from Cape Coral, who had stopped and then attempted to turn left onto SR 681. At the same time, the motorcycle was traveling south on SR 681 toward a yellow flashing traffic signal. The van proceeded into the intersection and into the motorcycle’s path, leading to a collision. The motorcyclist died at the scene, and investigators said the case remains open. (Sources: Cape Coral Breeze report, and a separate recap from WWSB MySuncoast.)
Where is SR 681 and why intersections like this matter
State Road 681 is a heavily traveled connector in the Venice area, linking I-75 traffic patterns with local roads. Intersections involving left turns are a common setting for severe motorcycle crashes because drivers may misjudge speed, overlook a smaller profile, or make a turn during a narrow gap.
Why motorcycle crashes are often catastrophic, even at “normal” speeds
In a car crash, occupants have steel frames, airbags, and seatbelts. Motorcyclists do not. That difference is why impact forces that might be survivable in a passenger vehicle can become fatal on a motorcycle.
Safety research consistently shows helmets reduce the risk of death and traumatic brain injury. NHTSA summarizes multiple studies finding helmets reduce fatalities and brain injuries significantly. Florida law also addresses motorcycle headgear requirements and the limited exception for riders over 21 who carry certain coverage.
What investigators typically examine after a fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident
When the Florida Highway Patrol (or another agency) investigates a deadly crash, the goal is to determine how and why the collision occurred, not simply who was involved. In a left-turn scenario, investigators often focus on:
In many cases, the early narrative changes as evidence is collected. That is one reason families benefit from preserving information quickly, even while the investigation is ongoing.
Legal issues families often face after a fatal motorcycle crash in Florida
After a fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident, families are typically dealing with two tracks at the same time: the emotional process of loss and the practical reality of finances and responsibility.
Wrongful death claims in Florida
Florida’s Wrongful Death Act provides a path for certain survivors and the estate to pursue damages when negligence causes a death. The statute outlines how damages may be claimed and how beneficiaries are identified.
This is not about “putting a price” on a life. It is about accountability and financial stability when a family’s future has been altered in an instant.
Insurance complications are common in motorcycle cases
Many people are surprised to learn how different insurance can be in motorcycle wrecks. For example, the Florida Bar explains that standard auto PIP coverage generally does not cover injuries from motorcycle accidents, though some additional coverages may be available depending on the policy.
That reality can increase pressure on families because medical bills and related losses are not always buffered the way they might be in a typical car crash.
What families can do right now after a fatal Sarasota County motorcycle accident
Every situation is different, but these steps can help protect your family while facts are still being gathered:

article.title: Tractor-Trailer Driver Sought After Fatal Hit-and-Run Crash on I-4 in Hillsborough County
article.text:
Authorities are searching for a tractor-trailer driver involved in a fatal hit-and-run crash that claimed the life of a 26-year-old motorcyclist on Interstate 4. According to the Florida Highway Patrol, the crash occurred on April 1, 2026, around 5:45 p.m. near Thonotosassa Road.
What We Know About the Incident
- The crash occurred on Interstate 4 westbound near Thonotosassa Road in Hillsborough County, Florida
- The incident took place around 5:45 p.m. on April 1, 2026
- The victim was a 26-year-old man from Lakeland riding a motorcycle
- The motorcyclist was traveling at a high rate of speed
- The rider attempted to pass two tractor-trailers by lane-splitting
- The motorcyclist lost control and fell from the motorcycle
- One of the tractor-trailers struck the motorcyclist
- The driver of the tractor-trailer fled the scene, continuing westbound on I-4
- The motorcyclist was pronounced dead at the scene
- The driver of the truck remains unidentified
- The Florida Highway Patrol is actively investigating
- Authorities are asking anyone with information to contact *FHP (*347)
Risks and Safety Concerns
Motorcycle crashes involving large commercial vehicles are often fatal due to the significant size and weight difference between the vehicles. Motorcyclists are particularly vulnerable and have little protection in the event of a collision.
Lane-splitting—riding between lanes of traffic—is especially dangerous and not legal in Florida. This maneuver reduces a rider’s margin for error and increases the likelihood of losing control or being struck by surrounding vehicles.
Hit-and-run incidents involving commercial vehicles present additional concerns, as fleeing the scene delays emergency response and accountability.
High-speed travel on busy highways like I-4 further increases the risk of catastrophic outcomes, particularly when combined with complex traffic maneuvers.
Legal Options and Potential Liability
Fatal crashes involving commercial vehicles and hit-and-run drivers often involve complex liability investigations.
Potential liable parties may include:
- Tractor-trailer driver: A driver who leaves the scene of a crash may be held liable for wrongful death and other damages.
- Trucking company: If the driver was operating within the scope of employment, the company may be liable under vicarious liability and for negligent hiring, training, or supervision.
- Multiple drivers: Liability may be shared depending on the actions of all parties involved, including the motorcyclist.
- Vehicle owner: The owner of the tractor-trailer may be responsible for damages caused by the driver.
- Third parties: If another factor contributed to the crash, additional parties may be evaluated for liability.
Under Florida’s comparative negligence system, fault may be apportioned among all parties involved based on their degree of responsibility.
In fatal crash cases, surviving family members may be entitled to pursue a wrongful death claim and recover compensation for:
- Medical expenses incurred prior to death
- Funeral and burial costs
- Loss of financial support and future earnings
- Loss of companionship and emotional suffering
Investigation Ongoing
The Florida Highway Patrol continues to investigate the crash and is working to identify the tractor-trailer driver who fled the scene. Authorities are expected to review traffic footage, witness statements, and any available evidence.
Anyone with information is urged to contact *FHP (*347) to assist in the investigation.
If you or a loved one has been affected by a similar accident, contact Flanagan & Bodenheimer Injury and Wrongful Death Law Firm for a free consultation to discuss your legal options.
article.author: Michael T Flanagan; Esq

article.title: Driver in Triple Fatal Car Crash Released on Bond
article.text:
The driver charged in the deaths of two teens and a good Samaritan who stopped to help them was released from Miami-Dade jail on bond Saturday.
Christopher Masferrer, 30, was arrested Friday and faces numerous charges including three charges each of reckless vehicular homicide and DUI manslaughter with failure to render aid, plus two other charges of DUI with serious bodily injury and DUI damage to property of person.
Masferrer's bond was posted at $28,500 before he was released, according to online jail records.
Police said Masferrer was behind the wheel of the SUV that hit and killed 17-year-olds Carolina Agreda and Anapaula Saldana along with 45-year-old Eduardo Hernandez.
Hernandez had just stepped out of his car when all three were hit. All three died at the scene of the crash. Hernandez’s father was in the car with him and witnessed the crash.
According to a police report, witnesses said Masferrer got out of his Kia, laid on the ground, and yelled, "What have I done?"
Officers who responded to the scene said several open and closed beer cans were visible in the front seat of Masferrer's Kia. According to the police report, a witness said Masferrer drank approximately 8 beers and one shot of an unknown liquor on the Saturday afternoon before the crash.
A toxicology report found Masferrer's blood alcohol level was recorded at 0.193 and 0.167, more than twice the legal limit.
The police report also stated that based on the car's black box, or airbag control unit, the Kia Masferrer was driving was going 71 MPH in a 40 MPH zone and hit the three people who died while driving 67 miles per hour.
The accident happened near the intersection of SW 56th Street and SW 157th Avenue.
On Saturday, friends and loved ones held a car wash fundraiser for Hernandez's family. They raised more than $10,000 that will go towards the education of Hernandez's children. Earlier this week, Hernandez's family also received a key to the City of Coral Gables, where he was an employee.

article.title: Motorcyclist killed in crash with FDOT vehicle on I-95 in northern Palm Beach County
article.text:
PALM BEACH GARDENS, Fla. — Drivers on Interstate 95 northbound in northern Palm Beach County faced significant delays Monday afternoon following a fatal crash that involved a motorcycle and a Florida Department of Transportation vehicle.
The wreck was reported at 12:47 p.m. just south of the Donald Ross Road exit, according to the Florida Highway Patrol.
Pictures from the scene showed the charred remains of an FDOT Severe Incident Response Vehicle.
The Florida Highway Patrol said the FDOT Severe Incident Response Vehicle was traveling northbound in the inside center travel lane. A 1980 Harley Davidson motorcycle, driven by a 41-year-old man from West Palm Beach man, was also traveling northbound in the inside travel lane.
The FHP said the 69-year-old driver of the FDOT vehicle didn't see the motorcyclist and began a left turn to travel onto a designated "Official Use Only" turnaround in the median of the interstate.
The FDOT vehicle traveled through the inside lane and entered partially onto the shoulder of the interstate.
The motorcyclist attempted to avoid the FDOT truck but couldn't avoid the collision, the report said.
The front of the Harley Davidson collided with the left side of the FDOT vehicle.
After the impact, the motorcyclist was ejected as the Harley Davidson went underneath the FDOT vehicle. The motorcyclist was pronounced dead at the scene.
The driver of the FDOT truck was not hurt, according to the crash report.
The FHP said they are still investigating the wreck.
article.author: Scott Sutton

DUNNELLON, Fla. (WCJB) - Authorities shut down a major roadway in Marion County as they respond to a deadly crash involving a bus on Tuesday morning. This developing story will be updated as more information is learned.
Florida Highway Patrol troopers say a bus with 53 people on board on State Road 40 between Ocala and Dunnellon around 6:30 a.m. The bus collided with a pickup truck for undetermined reasons causing it to veer off the road and flip.
Troopers arrested Bryan Maclean Howard, the driver of the pickup truck, on the charges of Driving Under the Influence – Manslaughter.
Troopers say the bus was carrying “migrant workers” to a watermelon farm. Eight people died in the crash and eight more are in critical condition. An additional 37 people were taken to the hospital with non-life-threatening injuries.
Authorities used a Marion County Public Schools bus to transport injured people from the crash to the hospital. No students were involved in the crash or hurt, the school bus was only used to help.
TRENDING: Elementary school principal arrested for aggravated child abuse, false imprisonment
SR 40 is closed between U.S. Highway 41 and West County Road 328. The closure is expected to last a long time as troopers investigate.
For the detour, drivers wishing to head eastbound on SR 40 should take US 41 northbound to West CR 328, then take West CR 328 eastbound back to SR 40. Driver wishing to head westbound on SR 40 should take West CR 328 westbound to US 41, then take US 41 southbound back to SR 40.
As far as he knows, Marion County Sheriff Billy Woods says everyone on the bus was legally permitted to be in the United States at the time of the crash.
“I get this question all of the time, about the individuals coming into the county. This is a very large county for your viewers who don’t know this, we are the fifth largest county in this state. We are a very big agricultural county. So, this time of year, we always have migrant workers that are on buses just like this.”
The bus was operated by Olvera Trucking Corporation, headquartered in Lehigh Acres, near Fort Myers. Federal records show the company’s three buses and two drivers have not been involved in crashes over the past two years.
No inspection records are available for the vehicles in the last two years. State records show their license as a ‘state contractor’ is good until next March.
Marion County Fire Rescue officials say the bus passengers were migrant workers on their way to “Cannon Farms.” The business posted on Facebook announcing it would be closed today out of respect for the “losses and injuries endured early this morning.”

article.title: Florida man rams into 2 law enforcement vehicles, crashes in canal and kills passenger
article.text:
Florida man rams into 2 law enforcement vehicles, crashes in canal and kills passenger
BROWARD COUNTY, Fla. (CBS12) — A woman who was riding as a passenger in a car that was fleeing from South Florida law enforcement was killed after the vehicle crashed into a canal on Sunday night, according to the South Florida Sun Sentinel.
The man behind the wheel, identified as Michael Malik Harvey, survived the crash and is facing charges of felony murder, aggravated fleeing and eluding causing death, aggravated assault on a law enforcement officer, grand theft of a motor vehicle and leaving the scene of a crash involving an occupied vehicle.
The South Florida Sun Sentinel says the events leading up to the crash occurred shortly before 7:30 p.m. on Sunday, when a Broward Sheriff's Office deputy saw a white GMC van driving recklessly on 33rd Avenue in West Park. The van was allegedly stolen from Miami-Dade County.
The Sentiel reports the BSO deputy turned on his lights to pull over Harvey, who was behind the wheel, but he did not stop. The sentinel reports that he allegedly hit the deputy's marked patrol car as he fled the area.
See also: Eyewitness who saw woman pushed from SUV remains key to solving 2007 murder
The deputy did not pursue Harvey in a chase, but instead remained at the scene of the crash, the South Florida Sun Sentinel states.
It wasn't until later that a Pembroke Pines Police Officer saw the van driving east on West Hallandale Beach Boulevard, west of I-95. Harvey later drove into the Oakwood Plaza area near Dania Pointe, where he “deliberately aimed the stolen vehicle at a marked police car, forcing the officer to take evasive action to avoid a collision,” the Sun Sentinel reports.
“A multi-agency pursuit” began for a charge of aggravated assault on a law enforcement officer.
When Harvey reached State Road 84 in Fort Lauderdale, he crashed through a closed metal gate while driving in the wrong direction and drove into a canal. BSO deputies, fire rescue crews and divers responded to the crash, rescuing Harvey and the woman passenger he was with.
Harvey and the woman were both taken to the hospital, the South Florida Sun Sentinel reports. Harvey is expected to make a full recovery, while the woman was later pronounced dead. The woman has not been identified as of Monday afternoon.
The Florida Department of Law Enforcement is investigating the “in-custody death,” while BSO detectives probe both the original West Park incident and the woman’s death.
article.author: Gershon Harrell

article.title: 70-year-old motorcyclist killed in crash involving 2 vehicles on US 41
article.text:
The Florida Highway Patrol is investigating a fatal crash involving a motorcycle and two vehicles on U.S. 41 in Lee County.
Troopers responded to the crash at around 5:17 p.m. on Monday at the intersection of U.S. 41 and Evergreen Road.
According to troopers, a vehicle driven by a 28-year-old woman from North Fort Myers was traveling west on Evergreen Road, approaching U.S. 41.
A motorcycle driven by a 70-year-old man from North Fort Myers was traveling north on U.S. 41, approaching Evergreen Road.
A third vehicle driven by a 55-year-old man from Fort Myers was traveling south on U.S. 41, approaching Evergreen Road.
The first vehicle entered the northbound lanes of U.S. 41 in front of the approaching motorcycle.
The motorcycle collided with the side of the vehicle, killing the North Fort Myers motorcyclist.
Following the collision, the first vehicle veered across the southbound lanes of U.S. 41, hitting the third vehicle and causing minor injuries to the North Fort Myers woman.
The crash remains under investigation.

article.title: US 1 reopens in Florida City after fatal crash involving motorcycle, SUV - WSVN 7News | Miami News, Weather, Sports | Fort Lauderdale
article.text:
FLORIDA CITY, FLA. (WSVN) - - A deadly crash in Florida City temporarily shut down U.S. 1 in both directions in the middle of rush hour.
Aerial cameras captured a motorcycle and a white SUV with visible damage at the entrance of a Wendy’s restaurant on Mile Marker 126, near the 18-Mile Stretch, at around 5:30 p.m., Friday.
A yellow tarp could be seen on the grass next to the Wendy’s sign.
Authorities shut down the roadway while they investigated. It has since reopened it to traffic.
Monroe County Sheriff’s Office deputies and the Florida Highway Patrol are investigating the crash.

article.title: Pickup collision with semi kills 1, injures 1 near Cross City
article.text:
A Cross City woman died and a Cross City man was seriously injured Tuesday afternoon when a tractor-trailer pulled out in front of their pickup on State Road 55 in Dixie County.
According to a Florida Highway Patrol (FHP) release, the 77-year-old Cross City man was driving his pickup northbound on SR 55 at 4:05 p.m. when a 38-year-old Alberta, Alabama man driving a semi pulling a flatbed trailer stopped at a stop sign on NE 132nd Avenue and then pulled out in front of the pickup, attempting to cross the northbound lane in the pickup’s right of way.
The front of the pickup collided with the left side of the trailer and both occupants sustained serious injuries. Dixie County Fire Rescue, with assistance from the Dixie County Sheriff’s Department, were on scene. Emergency responders transported the two Cross City people to UF Health Shands Hospital in Gainesville where the female passenger was pronounced

article.title: Man dies in crash on S.R. 19 in Lake County
article.text:
A 20-year-old man died in a crash in Umatilla Sunday afternoon, officials said.
Ryan Squitieri, of Eustis, was killed when his 2004 Kawasaki motorcycle struck the left side of Andenescia Walters’ 2006 Chevrolet van along State Road 19 at about 3:25 p.m., according to the Florida Highway Patrol.
In the moments leading up to the accident, Walters was stopped at a stop sign westbound on McKinley Road, which intersects with State Road 19, the FHP said.
Squitieri, who was wearing a helmet, was traveling northbound on SR 19 when Walters attempted to cross the road to enter the median of SR 19, according to the highway patrol. Squitieri hit the left side of the car.
Squitieri was taken to Florida Waterman Hospital in Tavares.
The highway patrol is still investigating the accident.
Charges are pending.

article.text:
Fatal crash closes north, south lanes of US 301 in Marion County, FHP says Investigation underway between CR 318 and SE 175th Street.
FHP logo (Graham Media Group)
MARION COUNTY, Fla. – A fatal Marion County crash closed both northbound and southbound lanes of US 301 on Wednesday afternoon, according to the Florida Highway Patrol.
Troopers said the wreck occurred at 1:00 p.m. on US 301 at SE 175th Street.

A 40-year-old man from Citra who was walking north on the eastern shoulder of US 301 suddenly darted into northbound lanes and was struck by a pickup truck, troopers said.
The pickup truck veered left into southbound lanes and collided with a tractor trailer, and the Citra man was pronounced dead at the scene, the FHP said.
The fatal accident is currently under investigation, and troopers caution drivers to avoid the area if possible.

article.title: Fatal Accident Closes Shula Expressway at Snapper Creek
article.text:
A fatal accident has closed all lanes on a portion of the Don Shula Expressway and others on the Snapper Creek Expressway Wednesday night.
Debris removal from the crash will take about two hours in the northbound lanes and three hours in the southbound lanes, the Miami Dade Expressway Authority said at 9 p.m. Wednesday night.
The closure is in effect on all lanes of the Shula/SR 874 between SW 104th Street/Killian Parkway and SR 878/Snapper Creek Expressway.
All westbound lanes on SR 878/Snapper Creek Expressway at SW 87th Avenue/Galloway Road are also closed.
The accident occurred on the Shula about 6:30 p.m. near the Snapper Creek Expressway when a truck overturned from the northbound lanes to the southbound lanes. One victim was pronounced dead at the scene and another transported to Baptist Hospital.
Drivers are urged to avoid the area, MDX says.
article.author: Janie Campbell

article.title: 2 Killed In Orlando Crash Identified
article.text:
ORLANDO, Fla. – Two people were killed and two others suffered serious injuries early Friday when a car crashed into a pole in Orlando, according to police.
Tastarian T. Benjamin, 22, and Jarvis T. Reynolds, 24, were killed in the crash, which happened around 12:30 a.m. on West Princeton Street near Orange Blossom Trail.
Recommended Videos
The driver, Shavonni A. Hemenez, 24, and Antoine Devon Davis, who turned 26 on Friday, suffered serious injuries.
According to Orlando police, the car was headed eastbound on Princeton Street when it lost control near OBT and slammed into a cement utility pole.
Charges are pending against the driver. Alcohol was not a factor in the crash, police said.

article.title: Child dies, 13 other people in critical condition after Marion County crash: FHP
article.text:
MARION COUNTY, Fla. - A child has died following an early morning crash in Marion County that involved a motorhome, according to the Florida Highway Patrol.
Troopers said the crash happened around 5:45 a.m. in the area of US 27 and CR 316. Officials said an SUV collided with a motorhome, causing the motorhome to overturn.
The motorhome, whose occupants were from Ohio, had a total of 15 people inside the vehicle, according to troopers. A 5-year-old was pronounced dead on scene, according to a release. Twelve others were transported to an area hospital in critical condition, including eight children, according to a news release.
The driver of the SUV was also transported to an area hospital in critical condition, according to officials.
The crash remains under investigation.

article.text:
PAGE 1

Section B: 2019 Delinquent Tax List A Madison man succumbed to his injuries after a single vehicle crash in Gilchrist County, Fla., on Sunday, May 17. The Florida Highway Patrol is currently investigating the single vehicle, single occupant traf c fatality which occurred on US Highway 129 at County Road 307 just south of Bell, Fla. A white Buick Sedan left the roadway and struck a tree resulting in fatal injuries to the driver who, according to sources, has been identi ed as Gazzara Purnell King, a major in rank at Lancaster Correctional Institution, in Gilchrist County.FATAL WRECK During a special/emergency session held just days after suspending town clerk Kim Reams pending the nal outcome of an intent to terminate, the Greenville Town Council made an offer to a Jacksonville certi ed personal accountant to ll the vacancy. Just one day after, a contract was made effective on Friday, May 15, and signed on Sunday, May 17. The virtual Zoom meeting commenced a little after 2 p.m. on Thursday, May 14, to consider/hire an interim town clerk. This meeting was necessary due to action taken on Monday, May 11, when a preliminary resolution to terminate Reams' employment with the Town of Greenville was passed 4-1. While Reams’ is still on the payroll, she now has less than 10 days to request an appeal or accept the termination. Meanwhile, it was announced during the May 14 meeting that deputyclerk Carolyn Spears submitted her letter of resignation the previous day, which left no one to run the of ce of town clerk and care for day-today administrative tasks, including payroll. "We have been reaching out to several entities regarding trying to nd someone that could come in and help us during this time," said Mayor Brittni Brown, in regards to searching for an interim town clerk. "Our town manager has reached out to the [Florida] League of Cities and the clerks association – some of the members of the board have also reached out to professional contacts that we may have, trying to solicit someone that can serve in that interim position." Lee N. Jones Jr., CPA, was the only candidate to submit a resume to the board, and spoke on his career, strengths and accomplishments. Gaining a bachelor's degree from the University of North Florida, Jones has over 25 years of experience growing, analyzing and managing businesses and departments, as stated in his resume. The document continues to state that Jones has experience working with public and private entities, federal and state agencies, grant administration and management as well as other entities. Since 1995, Jones has experience with a private practice (1995-2003), and has served as a chief nancial of cer for Community Investment Services of Atlanta (20032012), chief nancial and compliance of cer for First Anguilla Trust Company of The Valley, Anguilla (2012-2015), chief nancial of cer of The West End Project/(Community Based Organization Development Center, Inc.), of Atlanta (2015-2018) and chief nancial and operation of cer of Bat Conservation International (20182020). He is currently practicing privately once more, as his resume lists, providing nancial and technology solutions for nonpro t entities. "One of my strengths I'm hoping to bring to Greenville in transition here is not only nancial experience and training, but also my experience and training in technology and leveraging technology so that things become more ef cient, easier and ultimately cost less," said Jones while speaking to the board and interested parties through Zoom. "I really just want to do a good job, if I'm able, to ensure no critical services or no critical matters fall short while you're going through your transition." Before opening up discussion, Councilman Calvin Malone made a motion that the board consider hiring Jones as the interim town Greenville offers $87,000 to interim clerk After consulting with parents, school administration and health department of cials, James Madison Preparatory High School (JMPHS) has announced their plans for graduation ceremonies for the Class of 2020. The JMPHS graduation ceremony for the Class of 2020 is set to take place on Saturday, May 23, at 10 a.m. The ceremony will take place at Lanier Field. According to JMPHS Principal Mark Akerman, the ceremony was planned with the health and safety of both students and families as the top concern. All attendees to the ceremony are encouraged to wear face masks. If an attendee does not have a mask, one will be provided. If a mask is brought to the ceremony, it may not have any words or pictures on it. Graduating students will be admitted through the Crane Avenue entrance to Lanier Field at 9 a.m. Students are asked to park in the school parking lot and walk along Crane Avenue to the eld. Students are asked not to park in Lanier Field parking. They will be staged at the bottom of the entrance steps inside the gate. Staff will be on hand to assist and to help enforce social distancing rules. Students will only be admitted at this time. Parents and guests will be admitted through the Turner Davis Drive entrance near O'Neal's into Lanier Field as a group between 9 a.m. and 9:45 a.m. Parent groups will be escorted to their designated seating area. Family members must stay in their designated area throughout the entire ceremony. Families should come to the eld in one vehicle. Guests may park anywhere below Lanier Field, but are asked to leave the school parking lot open for students. Once inside, students will be escorted onto the eld by faculty and staff. They will be seated individually on the eld between the 20-yard lines. Students will be placed at least six feet apart. Parents and guests will be seated as visually close to their graduate as possible. Each graduate will be provided ve wristbands for family After a great deal of discussion with parents, school administration and health department of cials, Madison County High School has announced their plans for graduation ceremonies for the Class of 2020. At MCHS, the graduation ceremony has been set for Friday, May 22, at 7 p.m., at Boot Hill Stadium, on the MCHS campus. Seniors and their families are reminded that during the ceremony all attendees, including parents and guardians will assume the full responsibility for following all guidelines for social distancing, in accordance with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Anyone failing to follow those guidelines will be subject to removal. If anyone has a weakened immune system, they should contact Rashaunda Mitchell, the MCHS nurse. Prior to the ceremony, each senior will be given two color-coded parking passes (blue, green, yellow and red). These parking passes will correspond with the seating assignments for each senior. These parking passes will have the senior's name and number. The senior MUST ride in one of these two vehicles and both vehicles are to arrive at the same time. Gates at MCHS will open at 5:45 p.m. Seniors and their families must arrive no later than 6:40 p.m. in order to allow attendees to be seated in his/her appropriate seating. Late arrivals will not be permitted on the eld after 6:50 p.m. Late arrivals will be seated in the visitor side bleachers. Families seated in the blue section will enter through the MCHS gate and drive to the football eld, where they will be directed to the blue parking area near the eld house. The MCHS gate will close at 6:40 p.m. Families seated in the green section will enter through the main gate, then turn left and follow the roadway around and enter the gate going to the baseball/softball elds. Upon entering the gate, the family will be directed to the green parking area near the softball eld. Families seated in the yellow section will enter through the main gate, then turn left and follow the roadway to the JMPHS Graduation plans setMCHS Graduation plans set See “$87,000” on page 3A See “MCHS” on page 4A See “JMPHS” on page 4A Gazzara Purnell King "[The] majority of citizens in Greenville live below the poverty level and this is how this council is spending your money. Remember in November, Greenville.” -Bobby Burnett "I have no relationship with him, but I would like for you, though, to consider we are in an emergency situation." -Calvin Malone "Before the election, I went to my church, and other churches, and preached: 'Please don't change the people that are already [on the board]; if you do, we will go right back to where we were ' Those people up there, not all of them, they are not for the citizens." -Elesta Pritchett former Councilmember Councilmember Councilmember 

PAGE 2

Honoring those who died in the service of our country is at the heart of Memorial Day. Its origins trace back to the Civil War and involved decorating the graves of soldiers who fought and died in what some have called the central event in America's historical consciousness. Honoring the dead has been a challenge with the COVID-19 pandemic. Around the world people are facing limitations unlike any we have known in the care of persons who have died, not just from the pandemic, but during it. Coming together in familiar ways of encouragement to honor the life of one we have loved and lost is not responsibly possible, so we are nding creative virtual ways of remembering and comforting for now. Big Bend Hospice, which is known for its bereavement care, is offering a Virtual Memorial Day Service this year to pause and remember those who have died in service to our country. This sensitive program, acknowledging all branches of the military with beautiful music and carefully selected words, invites us to call to mind the loved one(s) we are remembering with appreciation for who they were and for what they sacri ced. As the Veteran Liaison at Big Bend Hospice, I invite the community to join us for a virtual Memorial Day Service lmed in our Veterans Memorial Garden at the Jean McCully Family House. Visit our web site or follow us on FB on or after Monday, May 25 to view. You might consider a virtual gathering with your family to share the viewing so that it becomes more personal to the intimate circle of the one(s) being remembered. If you want more information or help for viewing, please contact me at (850) 556-0229 or by email: . BBH holds virtual Memorial Day ServiceI'm currently reading Ian Toll's "Paci c Crucible" (2012, W.W. Norton, New York). It is the story of 1942 in the Paci c and the reversal of fortunes. The second half of 1942, or more speci cally June 1942 to January 1943, was the turning point in the Second World War. In the theaters of the Paci c, North Atlantic, Northern Africa, and Russia, the war turned against the Axis and in favor of the Allies. Something like 80 percent of Earth's surface is covered by water. Of all the "ponds", there are two big ones (discounting the Indian Ocean): the Atlantic and the Paci c. Between the two, the Paci c Ocean is about three times the size of her smaller cousin. Shortly after the Tripartite Pact joined the fortunes of Germany, Japan, and Italy into the Axis, American war planners began to look at the strategic implications of theaters and adversaries. In November 1940, more than a year before the attack on Pearl Harbor, they concluded that our principal threat was from Nazi Germany and that our primary theater of operations would be Europe and the Atlantic. They concluded it was absolutely vital to keep Britain actively engaged in the war and supplied. In the case of war in the Paci c, we would ght a defensive battle until the primary threat was overcome. Japanese society and strategy throughout the 1930s is an interesting study. The society was extremely militaristic and unsettled. Assassinations and changes in government were commonplace. In 1931, in part to give Japan's army something constructive to do, they invaded China. By the time that 1940 rolled around, America was quite sympathetic to the plight of the Chinese who were being systematically brutalized by the Japanese. Japan had four Paci c adversaries to contend with: Australia, the British in Singapore and Malaysia, the Dutch in Borneo and Indonesia, and the Americans, with their rival eet and base in The Philippines. Imperial Japan could probably contend with the rst three, but the Americans and their Paci c Fleet, now forward based from the West Coast to Hawaii, were worrisome. Negotiations between America and Japan centered on China – we insisted they withdraw from their conquests. Japan refused, so we began to squeeze them with embargoes on economic and war materials, rst iron and then oil. Oil was the long pole in the tent; Japan had no petroleum reserves of her own and was forced to import all of its fuel. In order to fuel their economy and war machine, they needed oil and the United States was standing in the way. In the early summer of 1941, Japan faced the decision of where to advance to solve their strategic dilemma: north into Russia, presenting Stalin with a two front war; or south toward the oil elds of the Dutch East Indies. The decision makers chose the southern strategy. That meant that the American and British bases in the Southwest Paci c had to be neutralized so that the sea lines of communication would be clear. The Imperial Navy chipped in a plan to neutralize the hundred ships of the Paci c Fleet by attacking them at anchorage in Pearl Harbor. D-Day was set for Dec. 8, 1941 in Tokyo where Japan's army and navy would simultaneously attack Allied targets in The Philippines, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand, Wake Island, Borneo, Indonesia and Hawaii. The attacks and subsequent occupation would require 90 percent of Japan's military strength. It was a huge gamble! Of course, the problem that confronted Japan after their initial successes was they had "bitten off more than they could chew." Barely six months after the attack on Pearl Harbor, Japan's fortunes were reversed, rst at Midway (June 4, 1942) and two months later at Guadalcanal. When you think of the four-year long Paci c War and that immense ocean, you imagine great navies and desperate battles at sea. In fact, from a strategic sense, the war was largely waged in the skies above and in the depths beneath the ocean -the Paci c War was primarily an air war and a submarine war. Because of inter service politics, we divided our forces into two theaters: Nimitz's Central Paci c and MacArthur's Southwest Paci c. Most of the invasions and land battles were fought to secure air bases. Both commanders bypassed Japanese strongholds where an air base was unnecessary, leaving a neutralized enemy behind to starve. Meanwhile, the Paci c Fleet's submarine force was tightening the noose around Japan, destroying her precious supplies bound from the South Paci c. Now 80 years later, Japan is a Paci c ally and China is our principle threat. The immediate threat is the coronavirus which originated in Wuhan and is wreaking havoc on world health and economy. National Security: The Paci c War JOE BOYLES I just read an essay published by Time titled, “What Jesus Really Said About Heaven and Hell.” It was adapted from a book by Bart D. Ehrman, once a self-professed born-again, fundamentalist Christian, now turned agnostic atheist. The book is titled, “Heaven and Hell: A History of the Afterlife.” Ehrman posits the Hebrew Bible, i.e. the Old Testament, does not teach that the soul of man survives physical death, but perishes with it. This unbeliever also says neither Jesus nor ancient Hebrews believed “departed souls go to paradise or everlasting pain.” When we talk about things like resurrection, heaven, hell, etcetera; we are basically talking about things that have to do with the afterlife. What the Scriptures mean are dependent on the subtle interplay of semantics and syntax. That is to say, what words mean, and what they mean when they are put together with other words in a sentence. Another way of saying this is what words mean in context. Ehrman delves into the etymology, the history of what a word means, and shapes the meaning of a passage of Scripture around that word rather than trying to understand how that word contributes to the context of the passage in which it is found. Ehrman delves into the etymology, the history of what a word means, and shapes the meaning of a passage of Scripture around that word rather than trying to understand how that word contributes to the context of the passage in which it is found. Take the term used in the Psalms, Sheol. The Psalms describe it as a person’s new location after death, but Ehrman says, “Sheol is simply a synonym for ‘tomb’ or ‘grave,’ It’s not a place where someone actually goes,” and “Jesus did not say souls would be tortured there. They simply would no longer exist.” Is that what Jesus said? Jesus tells a story about a rich man and a poor man named Lazarus. They both die and Jesus relates their story in the Gospel of Luke 16:19-31. The rich man ends up in Hades, a word borrowed from Greek mythology and is the equivalent of the Hebrew word Sheol in the Old Testament. Jesus said, “In Hades, he lifted up his eyes, being in torment, And he cried out and said, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus so that he may dip the tip of his nger in water and cool off my tongue, for I am in agony in this ame.’” That is what Jesus really said. Ehrman says Jesus did not say Sheol or Hades is a place “where someone actually goes,” but according to Jesus the rich man went there. Ehrman says, “Jesus did not say souls would be tortured there,” but Jesus, said the rich man was “in torment” and the rich man says he is “in agony in this ame.” Ehrman says people are consumed by the ames and “simply no longer exist.” Jesus did not say the rich man was consumed by the ames but was “in agony” in the ames. Never trust an atheist to tell you what Jesus really said. If you want to know what Jesus or the Bible really says about the afterlife then read it for yourself.Christian Concepts: Ehrman and the Afterlife GARY B. KING Our dog died last Wednesday. He had stopped eating and drinking and we knew the inevitable was coming. No matter how hard we prepared, we still weren't ready. There is such an empty hole in our lives. He has been here since the rst year David and I got married and he went to work with David almost every day. It's been a sad week at our house. My kids have cried. My husband is a wreck. And even though I miss the old dog, I'm over here doing what I always doholding us all together. This weekend, everyone went their separate ways and I decided to mow. I put my earphones in and got to work. As I made a turn to mow another pass, Zach Williams' song, "Heaven Help Me," came on. I've listened to this song so many times, but Saturday, there was one line that was louder than the rest, "I just need to hear you say that everything will be okay." I turned the mower off, propped my face in my hands, sat there and ugly cried. For the past eight months, it's been one emotional hit after another. Just the cancer journey alone is enough, but we have been hit from all sides at all levels. I'm tired. Weary. Worn. Exhausted. So, for a few minutes I stopped doing what I always do, and I just fell apart. I poured my heart out to God on that mower and cried big ole elephant tears until all of the dust and dirt left nasty streaks down my cheeks. I've been somewhat of a broken wreck most of the weekend, but sometimes the glue gets tired, too. Sometimes the "sticky" that holds it all together needs to loosen that hold, lean on someone a lot bigger, and regroup so they can get a better grip and hold it all together just a little bit longer and stronger the next time. I look down the road and I try to imagine a date or a time when all of the uncertainty of this year will be over. Longingly I reach for anything that appears stable, but right now there is absolutely nothing certain to hold on to. That's when I need to hear God say that everything will be okay. I need him to scoop me up in his big arms, hold me tight and assure me that the end is in sight. To not give up. To stay in the ght. To keep holding it all together. For just a little longer. Strong people get weak, too. We hold it all together until our "sticky" is pulled into a hundred different directions and nally loses its grip. I'm grateful for friends who love me when I've lost my "sticky". I'm also thankful for God who adds His "sticky" to the mix and holds it all together, always reminding me along the way, that everything will be okay. John Willoughby It's been said that retirement is wonderful if you have two essentials: much to look back on and much to live for. City of Madison's Natural Gas Superintendent Mike Smith, who is preparing to retire after 46 dedicated years, undoubtedly has an abundance of both. Smith will cap his career at the close of day on Wednesday, May 20. While his retirement had long been an item on his calendar, it was announced before the Madison City Commission on Tuesday, May 12. During that meeting, Smith was honored for his outstanding achievement. "The City of Madison is honored and proud to recognize your exemplary dedicated service through 46 years of employment," said city manager Jerome Wyche. "It is with unparalleled appreciation we thank you for your unwavering commitment, outstanding leadership and attentiveness as you have successfully guided the natural gas department to a level of excellence as the superintendent." Smith responded, calling his tenure a long haul. "I tell most of the young fellows that are being hired that I have been here longer than they are old," he laughed. "There's been a lot of challenges in the city, there's been a lot of hard work and there's been a lot of good times. It's been a pleasure working for the city." While four years shy of 50 years, Smith has worked with multiple generations and has made impressions on nearly everyone he's worked with. Jack Sealy, of Lee, worked with Smith for 29 years, before retiring himself. Sealy worked in the water department for two years before becoming a purchasing agent for the city. "I met Mike in '86," Sealy said, noting Smith's generous, kind and helpful persona. "Mike was an outstanding individual. He will do anything he can to help anybody." Water superintendent Johnny Webb, who is working on his 30th year with the City of Madison, says Smith is "a great friend to have around." Webb has worked closely with Smith on multiple projects. He states whenever water pipes were laid, gas was laid as well. "Mike helped me a lot over the years," Webb added. "The city's going to miss him." Smith, a devoted family man, is married to Judy Smith and has ve children: Josh Smith, Casey Andrews, David James, Brandon James and Nicole Hudson. Over the years, on the weekends and afternoon, Smith enjoyed spending time with his children and fourteen grandchildren. He currently has a fteenth grandchild on the way.
